title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: The team has significant concerns about this PR. Security_hawk flags a critical PCI-DSS violation (logging request bodies containing card data to console). Kiss_zealot objects strongly to the architectural over-engineering—a 6-row coupon lookup wrapped in abstract classes, strategy patterns, and a factory is textbook YAGNI and obscures rather than clarifies intent. Quality_critic finds pervasive naming/style issues (var, ==, console.log, meaningless names like `theData`, `tmp`, `doStuff`, `result2`), a silently-swallowed error handler, type-safety disabled with `:any`, and response fields in snake_case when the API uses camelCase. These aren't scattered nits—they cluster in the new code and erode trust in the implementation.

findings.title: Logging request body with PAN/CVC to console
findings.message: console.log of the full request body bypasses pino's redaction allowlist and exposes card data to stdout. PCI-DSS forbids plaintext PAN in logs. Remove this line or use the injected logger with proper field redaction. If this is debug code, it must not ship.

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger
findings.message: All logging should use the injected pino logger, not console.log. The codebase already has structured logging in place; bypassing it defeats redaction, log levels, and observability. Use `logger.debug()` or remove debug code entirely.

findings.title: Over-engineered coupon system for a 6-row lookup table
findings.message: An abstract class, two strategy subclasses, and a factory to select from six static rows is YAGNI—the abstraction exists for a hypothetical future, not a real problem. Replace with a simple data structure (a Record<string, { kind, value }>) and one `applyCoupon()` function. The current design adds three files and zero capability; when coupon rules *actually* get complex, generalize then with concrete examples in hand. For now, delete the abstraction and make the code smaller.

findings.title: Abstract class with only one abstract method should be interface
findings.message: AbstractCouponStrategy has no concrete methods or shared state—it's an interface masquerading as a class. Interfaces are the right tool here, and they don't force single inheritance. Use `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }` instead.

findings.title: Commented-out code (FREESHIP coupon)
findings.message: Dead code commented-out in version control rots. Git remembers the history; delete the comment. If FREESHIP is needed later, the version log will show it. Commented branches only create confusion for future readers trying to understand what's live and what's deferred.

findings.title: Function name 'doStuff' is meaningless
findings.message: An exported function called `doStuff` tells the caller nothing. Rename to something honest about what it does—e.g., `applyCouponDiscount` or `calculateDiscountedAmount`. A good name reads like documentation.

findings.title: Meaningless variable name 'theData'
findings.message: `theData` is placeholder naming—vague enough to apply to almost anything, meaningless in the context of a `charge` handler. Use a name that says what the data is: `const body = req.body;` or `const payload = req.body;` (then parse it). Names are the cheapest documentation.

findings.title: Empty catch block silently swallows errors
findings.message: A catch with only a TODO means any error from `doStuff` (bad coupon, type mismatch, exception) disappears silently. The discount silently becomes zero, and no one logs the failure. Either handle the error (log it, set a default, return 400) or rethrow. Silently swallowing is dishonest to the caller.

findings.title: Using ': any' disables type safety on response
findings.message: `: any` is a surrender to the compiler. Instead of giving up, model the response type properly. Define a response interface that includes the transaction fields plus `discountAmount` and `couponCode`, then use that type. The compiler can then verify the shape before it ships.

findings.title: Response fields use snake_case; API uses camelCase
findings.message: The response mixes conventions: `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` are snake_case, but the rest of the API (and the transaction object) uses camelCase. Change to `discountAmount` and `couponCode` so the contract is consistent and consumers don't have to special-case this endpoint.

findings.title: Using 'var' instead of const/let
findings.message: This file uses `var x = ...` while the rest of the codebase uses `const` and `let`. Also, `x` is a meaningless name. Change to `const discounted = amount * (1 - this.p);` to follow the house style and be honest about what the variable is.

findings.title: Using '==' instead of '===' for equality check
findings.message: The codebase uses `===` for strict equality. Change `if (code == 'WELCOME10')` to `if (code === 'WELCOME10')` to match the project style and avoid loose-equality surprises.

findings.title: Using 'var' and meaningless name 'tmp'
findings.message: `var tmp = ...` uses `var` (should be `const`) and gives the strategy object a placeholder name. Change to `const strategy = CouponHandlerFactory.build(couponCode);` to follow const/let discipline and name the variable honestly.

findings.title: Using '==' instead of '===' for null check
findings.message: Use strict equality: `if (strategy === null)` instead of `if (tmp == null)`. Also, the variable should be named `strategy`, not `tmp`.

findings.title: console.log instead of structured logger
findings.message: Use the injected pino logger, not console.log. Also, logging unknown coupon codes to console bypasses the structured logging pipeline. Change to `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon code');` if this is worth logging at all.

findings.title: Logging user input to console
findings.message: User-supplied coupon codes logged to stdout is not a best practice. If logging is needed, use the structured logger with appropriate log levels, not console.log.

findings.title: Meaningless variable name 'result2'
findings.message: `result2` is a placeholder name—it doesn't say what the result *is*. This variable can be eliminated entirely: `return strategy.apply(amount);` on line 81. Or name it `discountedAmount` if the intermediate value is useful for clarity. Don't threadbare a name and immediately return it.
